coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive detection of breast cancer cells with a lectin-based electrochemical sensor using N-doped graphene quantum dots as the sensing probe
coredata.dc:creator: Tran, Hai Linh
Dang, Van Dien
Dega, Naresh Kumar
Lu, Sih-Min
Huang, Yu-Fen
Doong, Ruey-an
coredata.dc:description: In this study, an ultrasensitive electrochemical biosensor fabricated from the deposition of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) and phytohemagglutinin-L (PHA-L) onto screen-printed electrodes (SE) was successfully developed for breast cancer cell (MCF-7) detection in human serum. NGQDs with particle sizes of 3–12 nm were fabricated via a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method using passion fruit juice as the green carbon source, and then were conjugated with PHA-L through the amide bond between the COOH group of NGQDs and the NH2 group of PHA-L. The dual-functionalized NGQDs can enhance the electrical conductivity of the electrochemical sensor and serve as the nanocargo for PHA-L, a highly specific receptor for effective anchoring of MCF-7, and subsequently result in the ultrasensitive and highly selective detection of MCF-7. The prepared SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor exhibited a wide linear detection range from 5 to 106 cells mL−1 in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and 20–106 cells mL−1 in human serum with extremely low detection limits of 1 and 2 cells mL−1, respectively. Moreover, the superior selectivity over 6 different interferents and long-term stability after 80 d of storage in human serum make the NGQDs/PHA-L complex an excellent sensing probe that can electrochemically detect low concentrations of cancer cells for the early diagnosis and treatment of breast cancer and other malignant neoplasms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)
Breast cancer cell (MCF-7), phytohemagglutinin-L (PHA-L), electrochemically sensing probe

(a) Survey scan of the XPS spectra, the deconvoluted N 1 s spectra of (b) NGQDs and (c) NGQDs/PHA-L, and (d) FTIR spectra of NGQD-based nanomaterials. Curves A, B, and C in Fig. 2d represent the NGQDs, PHA-L, and NGQDs/PHA-L, respectively.
(a) Nyquist plot and (b) LSV current intensity of NGQD-based nanosensing probes, including pure SE, SE= |NGQDs, SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L, and SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L/MCF-7.

(a) LSV curves of the NGQDs/PHA-L sensor for the detection of various MCF-7 concentrations in PBS, calibration curves of the NGQDs/PHA-L sensor as a function of MCF-7 cell concentration in (b) PBS and (c) human serum, and (d) reliability of the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor synthesized from six different sources for MCF-7 detection at concentrations of 50–50,000 cell mL−1. The symbols and error bars are the mean values and standard deviations calculated from three replicates (n = 3), respectively.

(a) The LSV current intensity of the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor in the presence of MCF-7 and other interferents, (b) stability of the SE=NGQDs/PHA-L sensor cytotoxicity of NGQDs and NGQDs/PHA-L to (c) MCF-10A and (d) MCF-7 cells. The symbols and error bars are the mean values and standard deviations calculated from three replicates (n = 3), respectively.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive detection of breast cancer cells with a lectin-based electrochemical sensor using N-doped graphene quantum dots as the sensing probe

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, an ultrasensitive electrochemical biosensor fabricated from the deposition of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) and phytohemagglutinin-L (PHA-L) onto screen-printed electrodes (SE) was successfully developed for breast cancer cell (MCF-7) detection in human serum. NGQDs with particle sizes of 3–12 nm were fabricated via a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method using passion fruit juice as the green carbon source, and then were conjugated with PHA-L through the amide bond between the COOH group of NGQDs and the NH2 group of PHA-L. The dual-functionalized NGQDs can enhance the electrical conductivity of the electrochemical sensor and serve as the nanocargo for PHA-L, a highly specific receptor for effective anchoring of MCF-7, and subsequently result in the ultrasensitive and highly selective detection of MCF-7. The prepared SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor exhibited a wide linear detection range from 5 to 106 cells mL−1 in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and 20–106 cells mL−1 in human serum with extremely low detection limits of 1 and 2 cells mL−1, respectively. Moreover, the superior selectivity over 6 different interferents and long-term stability after 80 d of storage in human serum make the NGQDs/PHA-L complex an excellent sensing probe that can electrochemically detect low concentrations of cancer cells for the early diagnosis and treatment of breast cancer and other malignant neoplasms.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Breast cancer cell (MCF-7), phytohemagglutinin-L (PHA-L), electrochemically sensing probe

ce:section.ce:para: In spite of the advances in the prognosis and therapy of breast cancer treatment, the recurrence of tumors after radio- and chemo-therapy still occurs. The rapid detection of low-level cancer cells for the early diagnosis and therapy of cancer tumors is, therefore, of great scientific significance [1]. Breast cancer is one of the most common causes of cancer-related death for women, and the breast cancer cell line MCF-7 is a well-known luminal-A subtype of a breast cancer cell line [2,3]. Therefore, the detection of low concentrations of MCF-7 in human serum is crucial for the early diagnosis of breast cancer. Very recently, nanobiosensors using different detection techniques, such as colorimetric [4], electrochemiluminescent [5], photoelectrochemical [6], and electrochemical methods [7–9], have been developed for the rapid and accurate detection of MCF-7. Among them, the electrochemical biosensor is one of the most cost-effective and efficient devices that can identify MCF-7 due to its short response time, ease of use, high sensitivity, and wide detection range.

Several bio-recognized receptors, such as folic acid [10], DNA [2], aptamer [7,11], and concanavalin A lectin receptors [12,13], have been used for MCF-7 detection. These receptors can be anchored onto the surface of nanocarriers such as GQDs to specifically bond with the MCF-7 cell membrane [2,14]. However, such receptors are expensive yet have low specificity for the detection of MCF-7. Phytohemagglutinin-L (PHA-L) is an N-acetylgalactosamine/galactose sugar-specific lectin with a strong agglutinative effect on MCF-7; it has a strong bioaffinity and specificity to conjugate with MCF-7 because its tetrameric structure is highly specific to mono- and oligosaccharides and to the sialic acids of the surface sugars on the MCF-7 cell membrane [15,16]. Therefore, PHA-L lectin can serve as a specific receptor to attach to breast cancer cells and greatly enhance the specificity for MCF-7 cell detection.

Several metal nanoparticles (MNPs) and carbon-based nanomaterials, such as Fe3O4 [14], gold (Au) [17,18], silver (Ag) [7], copper (Cu) [6], graphene [2], and GQDs [19], have been used as sensing probes for the optical and electrochemical detection of MCF-7. GQDs are a class of zero-dimensional graphene-based carbon dots (CDs) with excellent optical and electrical properties, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and high photostability to enhance the sensitivity and selectivity in the detection of analytes such as heavy metal ions, organic pollutants, viruses, biomarkers and cancer cells [20–23]. Although the fabrication of GQDs from different carbon sources has been well described [19–23], the green synthesis of GQDs from natural carbon sources such as pinewood [24], tobacco [25], and fruit juice [26,27] for nanosensing is still a common research topic. In addition, the doping of GQDs with heteroatoms such as N, S, and B significantly increases the electron density on the surface network of the GQDs, resulting in the enhancement in sensing sensitivity toward the detection of a wide variety of analytes [26,28,29]. A previous study utilized different sources of passionfruit juice to fabricate B,N-codoped GQDs for tetracycline detection and found that the quantum efficiency and the analytical performance of B,N-GQDs is independent of the source of passionfruit [26]. Passion fruit is a natural product that inherently contains plenty of nitrogen (N) and carbon (C), and it is a green and environmentally benign material that can be used to fabricate N-doped GQDs (NGQDs) without the addition of extra chemicals. More recently, several studies have depicted that the modification of SE with carbon-based nanomaterials such as graphene and GQDs can increase the electroactive sites to obviously enhance the mass transfer rate and electrocatalytic activity of the electrodes, resulting in an increase in the electrochemical response for analyte detection [20,30–32]. To date, however, no related work has been attempted to fabricate natural product-derived NGQDs as nanocarriers for the electrochemical detection of MCF-7 in the presence of PHA-L.

Herein, a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method was developed to fabricate NGQDs from passionfruit as the green carbon material and then serving as the nanocarrier to conjugate with PHA-L for electrochemical detection of MCF-7 cells in human serum. As shown in Scheme 1, the NGQDs were fabricated at 170 °C for 20 min and then conjugated with PHA-L via the formation of amide bonds between the -COOH group of NGQDs and the -NH2 group of PHA-L. The NGQD/PHA-L complex is coated onto the surface of the SE to form the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor for MCF-7 detection using linear sweep voltammetry (LSV). The SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor also illustrates good biocompatibility, enhanced current response, high stability, and extraordinary electrocatalytic performance toward MCF-7 detection. Linear ranges of 5–106 and 20–106 cells mL−1 with limit of detection (LOD) values of 1 and 2 cells mL−1 in PBS and human serum, respectively, were observed. The selectivity of the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor toward interferents, including normal cells and other cancer cells, was investigated. Moreover, the cytotoxicity of NGQD/PHA-L on MCF-7 cells was examined. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on utilizing environmentally benign PHA-L as the targeting ligand for the electrochemical detection of the breast cancer cell line MCF-7 with a wide dynamic range of 5 orders of magnitude and an extremely low LOD value in human serum, which can trigger the development of a lectin-based sensing probe as a platform for the ultrasensitive and selective detection of MCF-7 and other cancer cell lines for the early diagnosis and therapy of diseases.

Fresh passionfruit was purchased at local markets in Hsinchu, Taiwan. PHA-L lectin from Phaseolus vulgaris was purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. Commercially filter-sterilized human serum AB was obtained from Gemini Bioproducts Co. Human breast cancer cells (MCF-7), a normal breast cell line (MCF-10A), immortalized human keratinocytes (HaCaT) and mouse embryonic fibroblast cells (NIH3T3) were purchased from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA). A SE with dimensions of 3.4 (L) × 1.0 (W) × 0.05 (H) cm3 was obtained from Metrohm Dropsens.

NGQDs were synthesized by microwave treatment by the carbonization of passionfruit juice [26]. The seeds and fragments of the passionfruit juice were removed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. After that, the supernatant of the juice mixture was harvested after filtration through a 0.2-μm membrane filter to remove suspended fibers and debris. Then, 20 mL of the supernatant was transferred into microwave tubes and heated at 170 °C for 20 min to produce NGQDs. Afterward, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min to separate the large particles, and then the soluble impurities were removed by using a 1k Da dialysis bag for 1 d with distilled water as the dialysate. The NGQD powders were harvested using freeze-drying for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the NGQD/PHA-L-based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The NGQDs/PHA-L-based sensing probe was prepared by mixing 0.1–0.42 mg NGQDs powder and 0.2 mg carbon black mixed with 5 µL of 5 wt% Nafion and 100 µL of 2-propanol in an ultrasonicator for 30 min to form a homogeneous paste. Nine microliters of the ultrasonicated sample was then drop-cast onto the surface of the working electrode [33]. The electrode coated with NGQDs was dried in air for 5 min. Then, 10 µL of 0.9–4.5 mg mL−1 PHA-L solution was added directly to the surface of the SE= |NGQDs electrode and kept for 1 h in air followed by thorough washing with DI water to remove the unattached PHA-L on the electrode surface. Finally, the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor was washed with DI water and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

The morphology and particle size of electrode materials, including NGQDs and NGQD/PHA-L, were examined with transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning TEM (STEM). The crystallinity, functional groups, and optical properties of NGQDs and NGQD/PHA-L were identified with X-ray diffractometry (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and UV–visible spectroscopy, respectively. Moreover, the carbon structure, elemental species, and fine structures of the electrode materials were characterized by Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS). The details of the characterization can be found in the supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MCF-7 with the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L electrode was incubated with MCF-7 at various concentrations of 0–106 cells mL−1 in PBS for 10 min at room temperature. After incubation, the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor was washed with 0.1 M PBS at pH 7.2 to remove the unbound MCF-7 cells, and then LSV analysis was performed. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was also used to elucidate the attachment of MCF-7 on the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor after 10 min. As shown in Fig. S1, the shape of the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L surface changed, and the thickness increased from 5 to 15 nm, which indicates that MCF-7 was successfully anchored onto the SE= |NGQDs/PHA sensor after 10 min

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MCF-7 with the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of using NGQDs as nanocarriers of PHA-L for MCF-7 detection in real sample applications, the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor was used to detect MCF-7 in human serum. The human serum was diluted 10 times with 0.1 M PBS at pH 7.2, and then various concentrations of MCF-7 ranging from 0 to 106 cells mL−1 were spiked into the diluted human serum for sensing. All measurements were carried out at least 3 times to evaluate the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the electrochemical sensor system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor was investigated by incubating the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor with different interferents, including AFP, TAU, PSA, HatCa, NIH3T3, HaCaT, MCF-10A, and MCF-7 cells. The concentration used for AFP, TAU, and PSA was 50 ng mL−1, while the added concentration of HaCaT, NIH3T3, MCF-10A, and MCF-7 cells was 106 cells mL−1.

The long-term stability of the biosensor was further evaluated from the analytical performance of the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor toward MCF-7 cell detection during different storage periods. The SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor was stored at 4 °C for 80 d, and the analytical performance of the sensor was examined every 20 d by using diluted human serum samples containing 106 MCF-7 cells mL−1 to investigate the stability of the biosensor.

The electrochemical performance of the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor was characterized by the LSV response of MCF-7 at different concentrations using 0.1 M PBS containing 0.1 M KCl and 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]4- as the electrolyte. The NGQDs/PHA-L in the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor served as the electron mediator, transferring electrons from the [Fe(CN)6]4- in the electrolyte to enhance the current response during LSV. The LSV signals were recorded in a window ranging from − 0.3 to + 0.7 V at a scan rate of 0.1 V s−1. Moreover, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were obtained within a frequency range of 0.1–10,000 Hz using a 0.1 M PBS at pH 7.2 containing 0.1 M KCl and 0.001 M [Fe(CN)6]4- as the electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of the NGQDs and NGQDs/PHA-L
ce:section.ce:para: The biocompatibility and toxicity of NGQDs and NGQDs/PHA-L toward MCF-7 cell viability were evaluated using the MTT assay before and after the loading of NGQDs/PHA-L on MCF-7 and MCF-10A cells. The viability of MCF-7 and MCF-10A cells was assessed by adding MTT reagent to solutions containing various concentrations (0, 50, 100, 150, 300, and 500 μg mL−1) of NGQDs/PHA-L in a 96-well plate (100 µL/well) containing 5 × 103 cells that were seeded and grown for 24 h at 37 °C in the presence of 5 % CO2. After incubating for 24 h, the MCF-7 and MCF-10A cells were washed with 0.1 M UPSB at pH 7.4 and stored in an incubator for another 24 h. The cell viability of MCF-7 and MCF-10A cells was estimated by a microplate reader.

In this study, the topography and morphology of NGQD-based nanomaterials were first examined by AFM and TEM. As shown in the AFM image ( Fig. 1a), the spherical NGQDs had heights distribution in the range of 0.5–1.5 nm with a mean height of 1 nm. These values correspond to 3–5 layers in thickness, indicating the successful synthesis of NGQDs from passionfruit juice. Moreover, well-dispersed NGQDs with particle sizes of 3–12 nm and an average diameter of 8 ± 1 nm are shown in the TEM image (Fig. 1b). After PHA-L was loaded onto the surface of the NGQDs, the thickness and shape of the NGQDs changed, and this change can be clearly seen as the white dots in Fig. 1c. The particle size of irregularly shaped NGQDs/PHA-L also increased to 230–250 nm (Fig. 1d). The STEM image of the NGQDs in Fig. 1e shows that the six-member carbon ring was uniformly arranged in a hexagonal honeycomb along with the (100) plane of graphene, which can be observed in the FFT image (inset of Fig. 1e). The lattice fringes were well defined with a d-spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. S2), which is consistent with a previous GQD report [34]. Moreover, the STEM image of the NGQDs/PHA-L signifies the presence of PHA-L on the surface of the NGQDs, which is consistent with its STEM image (Fig. 1f).

To estimate the elemental distribution patterns in the NGQDs, X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) and elemental mapping of NGQDs were performed. As shown in Fig. 1g, the EDS spectra and mapping of the NGQDs show that the atomic ratios of C, O, and N were 70.1, 19.5, and 10.4 at %, respectively. This result clearly indicates that a rich content of nitrogen atoms, which is inherently present in natural passion fruit juice, has been successfully doped on the graphene structure of the GQDs. The nitrogen atoms enhanced the optoelectrical properties of the NGQDs for electrochemical detection. Moreover, the XRD patterns of NGQDs exhibited peaks at 2θ values of 25.5° (002) and 40.2° (100), which are consistent with the values reported in previous studies (Fig. S3a) [26,33]. The carbon structure of NGQDs was further investigated by Raman spectroscopy. As illustrated in Fig. S3b, two characteristic peaks were observed at 1,358 and 1,584 cm−1, representing the crystalline D-band and graphitic G-band of NGQDs, respectively. The intensity ratio of the D to G bands (ID/IG) of 0.81 signifies the presence of sp2 carbon. Moreover, the broader peak of the D band is attributed to the presence of N atoms in the carbon backbone framework of GQDs [35].

XPS was performed to examine the change in elemental species in NGQDs before and after modification with PHA-L. Fig. 2a shows that the XPS survey spectrum of the NGQDs contained three major peaks corresponding to the O 1 s (532 eV), N 1 s (400 eV), and C 1 s (284 eV) core levels. The peak centered at 400 eV again proves the successful introduction of inherent N atoms to GQDs [36]. The C, O and N contents of the NGQDs, estimated from the XPS survey scan, were 65.5, 23.3, and 11.2 at %, respectively (Table S1), which are close to those indicated by the STEM-EDS results. After the modification of PHA-L, the survey scan of NGQD/PHA-L showed the increased peak intensity of the C 1 s spectrum at 284 eV. However, the peak intensities of the O 1 s and N 1 s core levels decreased, and the atomic percentages of C, O, and N changed to 69.3, 21.2, and 9.5 at %, respectively. This change is mainly attributed to the fact that the successful combination of PHA-L lectin and NGQDs increases the carbon content.

The deconvoluted O 1 s spectrum of the NGQDs contained three peaks at 530.5 (OC-OH), 531.5 (OH), and 532.7 eV (C-OH/C-O-C) (Fig. S4a), which confirm the presence of carboxylic groups and the hydrophilicity of the NGQDs [34,37]. The deconvoluted C 1 s spectrum of the NGQDs displayed the sp2 carbon peaks of CC/C-C (283.8 eV), CO (287.6 eV), and COOH (288.5 eV) (Fig. S4b). In addition, the prominent peaks corresponding to C-N (285.2 eV) and C-O/C-OH/C-NH (263.8 eV) indicated the successful doping of N atoms into the GQD network [26,37,38]. Furthermore, the N 1 s spectrum of the NGQDs shown in Fig. 2b contained peaks at 399.3 and 401.5 eV, which correspond to pyridinic- and pyrrolic-N, respectively [33,37]. After the modification of PHA-L with NGQDs, peaks in the deconvoluted O 1 s and C 1 s spectra (Fig. S4c and S4d) and the deconvoluted N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2c) shifted to the high binding energy range, which indicates the successful linkage of PHA-L with NGQDs. Further deconvolution of the N 1 s spectrum of the NGQDs/PHA-L also showed that the pyridinic- and pyrrolic-N peaks slightly shifted to 399.4 and 401.6 eV, respectively. In addition, the increase in pyrrolic-N peak intensity indicates successful binding between the -COOH group of NGQDs and the -NH2 group of PHA-L.

FTIR was performed to determine the presence of functional groups on NGQDs. As displayed in Fig. 2d, the broad peak of NGQDs at 3460 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of the O-H group. The peaks at 2,850, 1,702, 1,632, 1,430, 1,195, and 1,080 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of C–H, CO, CC, C–O–C, C–C, C–OH, and C–O groups, respectively [26,39,40]. The oxygenated functional groups indicated the hydrophilicity of the NGQDs. Moreover, additional stretching vibration peaks at 2935, 1310, and 630 cm−1 were evidence of N in the NGQD network, as they derived from N–H, C–N–O, and C–N groups, respectively. After modification with PHA-L, the peaks corresponding to the –NH and C–N on the NGQDs at 2935 and 630 cm−1 shifted to 2915 and 616 cm−1, respectively, and the disappearance and reduced intensity of the CO, C–N–O, and C-OH groups of NGQD again prove the successful interaction of the characteristic peaks between PHA-L and NGQDs [41].

XAS was performed to determine the local symmetry of the elements in the NGQDs. As presented in Fig. S5a, the C K-edge spectrum of NGQDs showed characteristic peaks at 286.7 and 288.5 eV, mainly attributed to the C 1 s-to-π * transition of CC and CO groups of the sp2 hybridized (graphitic) carbon. The sharp increase in peak intensity at 290.03 eV was typical of the C 1 s → π * transition due to the quantum confinement effect of the NGQDs [42]. In addition, the peaks at 294.0 and 300 eV were attributed to the C 1 s-to-σ * transition of the C-O and C-OH groups, respectively [42]. Similarly, the O K-edge spectrum was recorded in the 520–555 eV region (Fig. S5b). Two main absorption features located at 531.8 and 539–546 eV were clearly visible. The peak at 531.8 eV corresponded to the transition of O 1 s electrons to the π * state of the CO bond of the COOH group. Moreover, the broad peak in the region of 539–546 eV was assigned as the O 1 s-to-σ * transitions of C–O–H (539.5 eV), C–O (542 eV), or C-O-C (545 eV) groups. Furthermore, the N K-edge XAS spectrum of the NGQDs displayed the transitions from the N 1 s orbital to the π * states and the N 1 s orbital to the σ * states (Fig. S5c). The peaks attributed to the N 1 s → π * transition of the pyridinic-N and graphitic-N were positioned at 399.5 and 402.2 eV, respectively. Moreover, the N 1 s XAS spectrum exhibited N 1 s-to-σ * transitions in the high energy region of 407 and 413.8 eV, which are mainly attributed to the N-C and N-H groups, respectively [43].

After the successful fabrication and characterization of NGQDs, the electrochemical properties, including EIS and LSV, of NGQD-based SE were further investigated. Fig. 3a shows the Nyquist plot of the SE= |NGQD sensor in the frequency range of 0.1–10,000 Hz. The EIS spectrum usually contains a semicircle followed by a linear line, and the diameter of the semicircle, which can be defined as the charge transfer resistance (Rct), represents the electron transfer resistance between the electrolyte and electrode surface. The Rct of the bare SE was 890 Ω, but it decreased significantly to 5.5 Ω after NGQDs were coated on the SE surface (SE=|NGQDs); this change signifies the remarkable conductivity and low impedance of the NGQDs. The electronic interactions between the SE and NGQDs provide excellent electrical conductivity and catalytic surface area, which make the electrode extremely electroactive. When PHA-L was added to the SE= |NGQDs, the Rct increased again to 19.5 Ω, indicating the successful deposition of nonconductive PHA-L on the surface of the SE= |NGQDs. Moreover, the equivalent series resistance (Res), calculated from the horizontal intercept of EIS spectra, decreased from 19.8 Ω for pure SE to 1.36 Ω for SE= |NGQDs and 1.56 Ω for SE= |NQGDs/PHA-L, clearly demonstrating the low electrolyte transport resistance after the addition of NGQDs.

The addition of MCF-7 significantly increased the impedance and Rct of the electrode. Fig. S6 displays the EIS spectra of the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L electrode in the presence of various concentrations of MCF-7. The Rct and Res of the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor increased from 38.4 to 290 Ω and from 15.8 to 17.7 Ω, respectively, as the MCF-7 concentration was increased from 10 to 5000 cells mL−1. This result is attributed to the fact that the nonconductive MCF-7 hinders the electron transfer process and subsequently leads to an increase in the Rct and Res of the sensor.

To determine the influence of NGQDs and PHA-L on electrochemical performance, the added amount of NGQDs and PHA-L was optimized. Fig. S7 shows the change in LSV current as a function of the added amount of NGQDs and PHA-L. As illustrated in Fig. S7a, the current increased positively with the increase in NGQDs from 0 to 0.25 mg and then reached a plateau when the added amount surpassed 0.35 mg. In contrast, the addition of PHA-L slightly decreased the current because of its nonconductivity (Fig. S7b). The current remained stable when the PHA-L concentration was higher than 3.0 mg mL−1, clearly showing that the optimized values of NGQDs and PHA-L were 0.35 mg and 3.0 mg mL−1, respectively.

Fig. 3b illustrates the LSV signals of various NGQD-based sensors at a scan rate of 100 mV s −1 in the potential window of − 0.3–0.7 V. The LSV curve peaked at 0.22 V, and this peak was mainly attributed to the oxidation peak of Fe(CN)6 4-. The bare SE had a low current intensity of 0.11 mA, which then increased rapidly to 0.78 mA after the immobilization of NGQDs on the surface of the SE, corroborating the excellent conductivity of the NGQDs and their applicability to electrochemical sensors. The successful attachment of PHA-L onto SE= |NGQDs slightly decreased the current intensity to 0.7 mA. Finally, the current intensity decreased obviously to 0.4 mA when 5000 MCF-7 cells mL−1 were attached to the SE= |NGQD/PHA-L sensor, indicating that MCF-7 can effectively bind to PHA-L. These results clearly indicate that the developed SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor exhibits electroactive capability for MCF-7 detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the NGQDs/PHA-L-based sensor on MCF-7
ce:section.ce:para: After the successful fabrication and optimization of the SE= |NGQD/PHA-L sensor, the sensitivity and selectivity of the SE= |NGQD/PHA-L sensor on MCF-7 were evaluated. Fig. 4a shows the LSV measurement of the SE= |NGQD/PHA-L sensor at various concentrations of MCF-7 in PBS. The LSV current intensity decreased when the MCF-7 concentration was increased from 0 to 106 cell mL−1. The relationship between the LSV current intensity of the oxidation peak and MCF-7 concentration in PBS is shown in Fig. 4b. A two-phase linear relationship between the LSV current intensity and MCF-7 cell concentration was obtained. The first phase exhibited a linear relationship at low MCF-7 cell concentrations of 5–5 × 103 cells mL−1 (CLSV = 0.67 × CMCF-7 – 5.2 ×10−5, R2 = 0.999). Moreover, the relationship was highly linear when the MCF-7 concentration was within the range of 5 × 103 – 106 cells mL−1 with the equation CLSV = 0.41 × CMCF-7 –3.29 × 10−7 (R2 = 0.999).

Several studies have shown a 2-stage linear relationship when nanomaterials are used to detect a wide variety of analytes [20,44,45]. In this study, NGQDs served as nanocarriers that were conjugated with PHA-L and were then used as lectin-based sensing probes for the detection of MCF-7. Therefore, the attachment of MCF-7 onto NGQDs/PHA-L is a surface-mediated reaction. The molecular weight of MCF-7 is in the range of 430,000–600,000 Daltons, indicating that it may have steric hindrance after anchoring onto GQDs/PHA-L. This finding indicates that the reaction between MCF-7 and NGQDs/PHA-L is rapid when the concentration is low and then decreases as the MCF-7 concentration increases, which is similar to trends in other typical heterogeneous reactions [46,47]. Therefore, a 2-linear calibration curve is formed.

Human serum was used as the matrix to demonstrate the possible application of using the developed sensor in real samples. Similar to the analytical performance in PBS, the LSV current intensity decreased as the MCF-7 concentration in the 10 × diluted human serum was increased from 0 to 106 cell mL−1 (Fig. S8), and the SE= |GQD/PHA-L sensor also exhibited 2-line behavior in human serum, as shown in Fig. 4c. The first phase showed an excellent linear relationship in the low MCF-7 concentration range of 20–5 × 103 cells mL−1 (CLSV = 0.7 × CMCF-7 – 5.9 ×10−5, R2 = 0.997). Moreover, the second linear relationship at high MCF-7 concentrations of 5 × 103 – 106 cells mL−1, described by the equation CLSV = 0.4 × CMCF-7 – 3.35 × 10−7 (R2 = 0.997), was observed in human serum. The effect of the human serum matrix on the detection of MCF-7 cells was examined by comparing the slopes of the calibration curves in PBS and 10 × human serum. As shown in Fig. S9, the slopes of the calibration curves between PBS and human serum only showed a slight difference at low MCF-7 concentrations of 5–5000 cells mL−1 and were almost identical in the high MCF-7 concentration range of 5 × 103 – 106 cells mL−1. This result clearly indicates the high bioaffinity of the SE= |NGQDS/PHL-A sensor for MCF-7, and the effect of the human serum matrix has little influence on the detection of MCF-7 by the developed biosensor.

Several studies have used GQD-based nanomaterials on electrochemical sensors for the detection of a wide variety of analytes, and a two-phase linear calibration curve has been established [20,44,45]; these results are similar to the results obtained in this study. Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) of the SE= |NGQD/PHA-L sensor is estimated by 3σ/S, where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank signal (n = 7) and S is the slope of the obtained linear calibration curve [21]. The LOD was calculated to be 1 cell mL−1 in PBS and 2 cells mL−1 in human serum. The ability to detect MCF-7 cells at such a low concentration is imperative for the early prognosis and diagnosis of breast cancer. Moreover, the results observed in this study strongly highlight the promise of the developed SE=NGQD sensor for cancer marker detection.

To further elucidate the stability of the sensors, the NGQDs were prepared from different sources. Fig. 4d displays the LSV current intensity of NGQD-based electrodes fabricated from six different sources of passionfruit. The six prepared SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensors exhibited similar electrochemical performances, and a current intensity of 0.71–0.67 mA with an RSD of 1.8 % was observed. After the addition of MCF-7 at a concentration of 50–5 × 105 cell mL−1, the current intensity of all the NGQD sensors decreased with an increase in cell concentration. At a low MCF-7 concentration of 50 cell mL−1, the LSV current intensity of the sensor is 0.673 ± 0.012 mA and then decreased to 0.418 ± 0.008 mA at 5 × 103 cell mL−1, 0.391 ± 0.008 mA at 5 × 104 cell mL−1, and 0.221 ± 0.011 mA at 5 × 105 cell mL−1. The RSD of the current intensities of the six prepared electrodes ranged from 1.78 % to 4.97 %, clearly corroborating the excellent reproducibility of the electrochemical sensor developed in this study.

The reproducibility and sensitivity of the developed sensors were again investigated by adding an extremely low MCF-7 concentration of 5 cells mL−1 to the tested PBS solutions. As shown in Fig. S10, all seven electrodes yielded similar results, and a 1.6–2.3 % decrease in LSV current was observed when 5 MCF cells mL−1 was added. The decrease in current in the presence of 5 cells mL−1 was higher than the RSD of the currents of the bare SE (1.0 %), which signifies that the developed SE= |NGQD/PHA-L sensor possesses excellent reproducibility and stability in the selective detection of MCF-7 cells.

Several sensing methodologies, including colorimetric, photoelectrochemical, and electrochemical methods, have been developed for the detection of MCF-7 in various matrices [4,6,48]. The analytical performance of the sensing materials for MCF-7 detection is summarized in Table 1. Electrochemical biosensors composed of different nanomaterials, including Ag nanocrystals, Au-GQD@Fe3O4, and MoS2 nanoflower@gelatin loaded with various receptors, such as antibodies, aptamers, DNA, enzymes, and lectins, have been reported to be effective sensing probes for MCF-7 detection [7,8,12]. Usually, a dynamic range of 3–4 orders of magnitude with LODs of 1–367 cells mL−1 is observed. In this study, the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor displayed a wide linear range of 5–106 and 20–106 cells mL−1 with low LODs of 1 and 2 cells mL−1 in PBS and human serum, respectively, which is superior to most of the reported results. The wide dynamic range and low LOD obtained in this study are mainly attributed to the excellent electron transfer ability and high electrical conductivity of the SE= |NGQDs, subsequently resulting in the ultrasensitive detection of MCF-7 in both PBS and human serum. It is clear that the NGQDs used in this study serve as a nanocargo for PHA-L and an electronic conductor to enhance the electrochemical performance. PHA-L has a strong bioaffinity and specificity to conjugate with MCF-7 because of the tetrameric structure of PHA-L, which is specific to monosaccharides and oligosaccharides. Therefore, PHA-L exhibits a high affinity for the sialic acid on MCF-7 cell membranes, leading to increased sensitivity and selectivity toward MCF-7 detection [15,49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity and stability of the NGQDs/PHA-L sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor was further investigated by adding 6 different tumor markers and cancer cells to 10-fold diluted human serum. The interferents used in this study included 100 ng mL−1 AFP, TAU, and PSA and 106 cells mL−1 NIH3T3, HaCaT cancer cells, and MCF-10A normal breast cells. As shown in Fig. 5a, the current intensity of the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor in the presence of six interferents during the LSV experiments ranged from 85 % to 98 % that of the blank sensor in the absence of analyte. However, the current intensity decreased dramatically to 5.1 ± 0.2 % when 106 MCF-7 cells mL−1 were added, signifying the superior selectivity of the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor toward MCF-7 detection. Furthermore, the selectivity of the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor was evaluated in a mixture containing MCF-7 and other interferents, including normal breast cells, tumor markers, and cancer cells. A 95.1 ± 0.1 % decrease in current intensity was observed for the mixture, indicating that only 4.9 % of the current intensity remained, which is almost identical to that of pure MCF-7 cells. These results clearly corroborate that the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor is extremely sensitive and selective for MCF-7 cells in mixtures containing a wide variety of interferents, indicating the significant difference between blank MCF-7 and MCF-7 mixed with all interferents.

The stability of the SE=NGQDs/PHA-L-based sensor was further evaluated by assessing the analytical performance of the sensors after different storage periods. The sensors were stored at 4 °C first, and then some of the stored sensors were removed periodically and used to detect 106 MCF-7 cells mL−1 in PBS. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor retained 98.0 ± 2.4 % activity after 20 d of storage. The activity of the sensor decreased slightly to 91.6 ± 1.2 % and 88.7 ± 3.3 % that of the original sensor on the 40th and 80th days, respectively, highlighting the excellent long-term stability of the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L-based sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of NGQD-based nanomaterial to MCF-7 cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of nanomaterials, including NGQDs and NGQDs/PHA-L, was evaluated using MCF-7 and the normal breast cell line MCF-10A as the model cells. MCF-7 and MCF-10A cells were incubated individually with NGQDs and NGQDs/PHA-L. Fig. 5c shows the viability of MCF-10A cells on NGQDs and NGQDs/PHA-L in the nanomaterial concentration range of 50–500 µg mL−1. The viability of normal MCF-10A cells in the presence of various NGQD concentrations was in the range of (95.6 ± 8.8 %) – (103.2 ± 3.4) %, which indicates the low cytotoxicity of NGQDs on MCF-10A. After the modification of 50–150 µg mL−1 NGQDs with PHA-L, the viability of MCF-10A remained in the range of 99.6 ± 8.1–101.7 ± 4.9 %. The viability decreased slightly to (87.4 ± 6.3) – (93.6 ± 8.6) % when 300–500 µg mL−1 NGQDs/PHA-L were added. The viability of the MCF-7 cells in the nanomaterial concentration range of 50–500 µg mL−1 was 90.4 ± 2.2 % – 106.2 ± 5.7 % in NGQDs and 85.0 ± 2.0 % – 103.2 ± 2.7 % in NGQDs/PHA-L (Fig. 5d). The slight decrease in cell viability is probably due to the increase in the diameter of the NGQDs/PHA-L compared to that of MCF-10A cells and therefore leads to an increase in cytotoxicity [49]. Another plausible reason for the increase in cytotoxicity is the uptake of CDs by cancer cell lines [51,52]. A previous study indicated that cancer cell lines, such as MCF-7 and the human colon adenocarcinoma grade II cell line HT-29, can efficiently take up CDs, resulting in the labeling of cells with bright fluorescence emission from the cell membrane and cytoplasm [51]. These results also clearly elaborate that NGQDs and NGQDs/PHA-L exhibit low cytotoxicity in both normal and cancerous human breast cell lines and can be used as potential probes for the electrochemical sensing of MCF-7 cells.

In this study, we successfully fabricated an environmentally friendly SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor for the ultrasensitive and highly selective detection of MCF-7 in both PBS and human serum. The NGQDs generated from passionfruit juice had particle sizes of 3–12 nm and a mean diameter of 8 ± 1 nm and effectively served as nanocarriers for PHA-L to anchor MCF-7. A wide linear range of 5–106 and 20–106 cells mL−1 with low LOD values of 1 and 2 cells mL−1 in PBS and human serum, respectively, was observed. Moreover, the SE= |NGQDs/PHA-L sensor exhibited excellent selectivity for MCF-7 detection in the presence of normal breast cancer cells and other tumor markers. NGQDs and NGQDs/PHA-L have little cytotoxic effect on MCF-7 and normal breast MCF-10A cells. These results clearly corroborate that the electroactive NGQDs are superior sensing probes for the electrochemical detection of MCF-7 cancer cells in the presence of PHA-L, and this work provides a method of fabricating green and environmentally benign NGQDs/PHA-L from natural products as sensing platforms for the ultrasensitive and selective detection of MCF-7 and other cancerous cells.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hai Linh Tran received her PhD degree in Biomedical Engineering and Environmental Sciences from National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu, Taiwan in 2021 under the supervision of Professor Ruey-an Doong. Her research focuses on the fabrication of environmentally benign graphene quantum dot based nanocomposites for optical and electrochemical nano-sensing applications. She is currently serving as a postdoctoral researcher at the Institute of Nano Engineering and MicroSystems, National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu, Taiwan.

Van Dien Dang received his PhD degree in Environmental Engineering from National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University, Taiwan in 2021. His research focuses on the synthesis of nanomaterials for multifunctional applications such as sensing, photocatalysis, PEC water splitting, and supercapacitor.

Naresh Kumar Dega received his master degree from Osmania University, India. He is now a PhD student under the supervision of professor Ruey-An Doong in Department of Biomedical Engineering and Environmental Sciences, National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu, Taiwan. His research work mainly focuses on the development of nano-technology based optical and electrochemical sensing techniques for biological and environmental sensing applications.

Sih-Min Lu is graduated from National Taiwan Normal University, Taipei, Taiwan with bachelor degree in chemistry since 2019. He is now a master student in the Institute of Analytical and Environmental Sciences, National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu, Taiwan instructed by Professor Yu-Fen Huang. His master study mainly focuses on the nanocomposites based on transition metal chalcogenides for cancer theranostics.

Yu-Fen Huang received her Ph.D. degree from National Taiwan University, Taiwan. She is currently a full professor at National Tsing Hua University, and also serves as the Chairman of Interdisciplinary Program of Nuclear Science at National Tsing Hua University. Her research includes the development and design of multifunctional nanomaterials for targeted therapy and controlled drug release. She has published more than 100 scientific papers in the fields of biosensing, functional nanomaterials and cancer therapeutics.

Ruey-an Doong is a chair professor in the Institute of Analytical and Environmental Sciences, National Tsing Hua University, Taiwan. His current research interest includes the fabrication of nanomaterials with novel optical and electrochemical properties for biosensing of analytes, environmentally benign nanotechnology for treatment of contaminants, and porous materials for energy storage and conversion.
